title: Memory Addressing: LDR and STR
summary: Load data from memory into registers and store register values back to memory using LDR and STR.
content_html:
CPU Workflow with Memory

The CPU and ALU work only with values stored in registers (not memory directly). The standard workflow for operating on memory data is:

  1. Load value(s) from memory into registers
  2. Perform operations on values in registers
  3. Store result register(s) back to memory

LDR and STR Instructions

LDR (Load Register) copies a value from memory into a register.
STR (Store Register) copies a value from a register into memory.

LDR R0, 0xFC       ; R0 = memory[0x000FC]  (word at address 0xFC)
STR R1, 120        ; memory[120] = R1
STR R3, myLabel    ; memory[myLabel] = R3
LDR R4, myLabel    ; R4 = memory[myLabel]

Word Alignment

LDR and STR operate on words (4 bytes). The memory address must be word-aligned - divisible by 4.

LDR R3, 0x80    ; Valid  - 0x80 = 128, divisible by 4
LDR R3, 0x81    ; INVALID - 0x81 = 129, not divisible by 4
                ; The assembler will catch this error

Byte Addressable Variants: LDRB and STRB

For byte-level access (not requiring word alignment):

LDRB R3, 0x81   ; Load ONE byte from address 0x81 into R3
                ; Loaded into LEAST SIGNIFICANT 8 bits of R3
                ; Other bits of R3 are set to 0

STRB R3, 0x81   ; Store LEAST SIGNIFICANT 8 bits of R3
                ; into byte at address 0x81
                ; Neighbouring bytes unchanged

Indirect Addressing

Indirect addressing uses a register to hold the memory address. This is the basis of pointers in languages like C.

LDR R0, [R1]    ; R0 = memory[R1]  (R1 holds the address)
STR R3, [R4]    ; memory[R4] = R3  (R4 holds the address)

; Example: R1 = 0x200
LDR R0, [R1]    ; Loads the word at address 0x200 into R0

Defining Variables in Assembly

Labels define named memory locations. Use .word to allocate memory:

myVar:  .word 42     ; Defines 'myVar' holding value 42
arrayX: .word 1,2,3,4  ; Four consecutive words

Complete Example

; Load a value, double it, store back
myNum: .word 7

_start:
  LDR R0, myNum      ; R0 = 7
  ADD R0, R0, R0     ; R0 = 14  (double it)
  STR R0, myNum      ; Store 14 back to myNum
  HALT

exercises.title: LDR purpose
options: Copies a value from memory into a register
Stores a register value into memory
exercises.answer: Copies a value from memory into a register

exercises.title: STR purpose
options: Stores the value in R1 into memory address 120
exercises.answer: Stores the value in R1 into memory address 120

exercises.title: Word alignment
exercises.prompt: LDR/STR requires the address to be:

exercises.title: LDRB
exercises.prompt: LDRB differs from LDR in that it:
options: Loads only one byte, no alignment required
Loads two bytes at a time
exercises.answer: Loads only one byte, no alignment required

exercises.title: Indirect addressing
options: Loads the word at the address held in R1 into R0
exercises.answer: Loads the word at the address held in R1 into R0

exercises.title: Invalid address
